body:
# next-forge Skill Pack

A Claude Code skill that gives the agent real, current knowledge of the next-forge template. Without this, the agent often suggests patterns that apply to generic Next.js but break next-forge's conventions.

## Install

```bash
# as a local skill
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/next-forge/references
# (see the file layout below)
```

## SKILL.md

```markdown
---
name: next-forge
description: >
  Use when the user is working in a next-forge project — editing @repo/* packages,
  wiring new apps, configuring Turbopack, managing the monorepo's env var model,
  or debugging shared-types drift. Triggers on "next-forge", "@repo/auth",
  "apps/web", "why can't I import", "turbopack config", "env variable not picked
  up", "shared types". Skip for non-monorepo Next.js projects.
---

# next-forge

next-forge is Vercel's Turborepo-based SaaS starter. It splits a SaaS app into apps (web, app, api, docs, email, storybook) and packages (@repo/auth, @repo/database, @repo/design-system, @repo/payments, etc). The main reason it's tricky for agents: the imports between packages use the `@repo/*` alias which looks like node_modules but isn't, and the env var model is scoped per-app.

## The four mandatory phases

### Phase 1 — Identify the context

Read `references/layout.md` before editing anything.

Confirm:
- Which `apps/*` directory the edit is in
- Which `@repo/*` packages that app depends on
- Whether the change should be in the app or the shared package

### Phase 2 — Respect the package boundaries

Read `references/packages.md`.

Rules:
- Never reach across apps (apps/web importing from apps/app is broken)
- Package changes need a version bump if published externally
- Shared types live in @repo/database (Prisma) or @repo/... (domain-specific packages)

### Phase 3 — Handle the env var model

Read `references/env-vars.md`.

Each app has its own `.env.local`. Shared secrets go in root `.env`. Turbopack picks up env vars per-app — a var set in apps/web doesn't exist in apps/api unless you also set it there OR promote it to the root.

### Phase 4 — Don't break Turbopack caching

Read `references/turbopack.md`.

Turbopack is the bundler. Its cache keys include `package.json`, `turbo.json`, and the file contents of the apps/packages involved. Accidentally touching `turbo.json` busts the cache for the whole monorepo. Don't add globs that capture generated files.

## Common mistakes this skill catches

- Importing `@repo/auth` from a file in `packages/database` (circular dependency)
- Adding an env var to `apps/web/.env.local` that the API app also needs
- Editing `node_modules` thinking it's `@repo/*`
- Running `prisma generate` in the app directory instead of the database package
- Forgetting to bump `turbo.json` when adding a new task
- Using `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` for a server-only secret

## When NOT to use this skill

- Generic Next.js apps without Turborepo → use the nextjs skill
- Building a new monorepo from scratch → set up Turborepo directly, don't shoehorn next-forge
```

## references/layout.md

```markdown
# next-forge Package Layout

```
apps/
  web/              # marketing site (Next.js)
  app/              # application (Next.js)
  api/              # standalone API (Next.js)
  docs/             # documentation
  email/            # email templates (React Email)
  storybook/        # component stories
packages/
  @repo/auth/       # Clerk wrapper
  @repo/database/   # Prisma client + schema
  @repo/design-system/  # shadcn + tailwind + components
  @repo/payments/   # Stripe wrapper
  @repo/analytics/  # PostHog wrapper
  @repo/email/      # Resend wrapper
  @repo/feature-flags/ # flag definitions
  @repo/typescript-config/ # base tsconfig
  @repo/eslint-config/     # shared eslint
  ...
```

The rule: if a thing needs to be used by 2+ apps, it lives in `packages/`. If it's only for one app, it lives inside that app.
```

## references/packages.md

```markdown
# next-forge Package Rules

## Import syntax

- `import { auth } from '@repo/auth'` ✓
- `import { auth } from '../../../packages/auth'` ✗ — breaks cache and aliases
- `import { auth } from '@repo/auth/server'` ✓ (subpath)

## Type flow

- Prisma types are re-exported from `@repo/database`
- Auth types come from `@repo/auth`
- Shared domain types live next to their owner, not in a global types package

## Don't do this

- Don't add a dep to `apps/web/package.json` without also checking if it should be in a shared package instead
- Don't import server-only code into a client component (use `server-only` package)
- Don't circular-import between packages
```

## references/env-vars.md

```markdown
# next-forge Environment Variables

## The three levels

1. **Root `.env`** — shared across the monorepo. DATABASE_URL lives here.
2. **App `.env.local`** — app-specific. NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL differs per app.
3. **Turbo per-task globalEnv** — declared in `turbo.json` so Turbopack cache picks them up.

## The gotcha

If a var is set in root `.env` but not in `turbo.json` `globalEnv`, Turbopack won't invalidate cache when it changes. Symptom: changing DATABASE_URL doesn't seem to do anything.

## NEXT_PUBLIC_* prefix

All client-exposed vars must be prefixed. Server-only secrets must NOT be prefixed. Every next-forge app's `env.ts` file documents which vars are which.
```

## references/turbopack.md

```markdown
# Turbopack rules in next-forge

## Cache key

- turbo.json contents
- package.json files in apps and packages
- Source file contents via glob
- env vars declared in globalEnv

## Common cache invalidation pitfalls

- Adding a file to an input glob bumps the cache of every task that uses that glob
- Adding a new env var to globalEnv invalidates every downstream task
- Changing the dev script doesn't invalidate — dev tasks are cache-bypassed by convention

## When to clear

- `npx turbo run build --force` bypasses cache
- `rm -rf .turbo node_modules/.cache` for a full nuke
- CI should always use `--force` on main branch deploys to avoid stale-cache bugs
```
